moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with this flexible knight move, which is the hallmark of the Zukertort Opening—a system favored by strong positional players who want to avoid heavy theory. The knight controls e5 and d4, key central squares, while keeping our options open for how we'll build our center. Unlike 1.e4 or 1.d4, this move doesn't immediately claim space, but it prepares to do so gradually while maintaining flexibility in our setup.

Black stakes a claim in the center with this aggressive pawn thrust, immediately challenging our influence. This move is characteristic of Black's ambitions in the opening—it controls e4 and gains space, but it also weakens Black's kingside and the e6 square, which we should keep in mind for future attacks. Black is signaling they want dynamic play rather than a quiet position.

We strengthen our control of e4 and prepare to advance in the center methodically. This move also supports a potential f4 push if we want to contest Black's space, and it opens the diagonal for our light-squared bishop on c1. By playing d3 instead of immediately attacking with e4, we're being patient—we want to provoke weaknesses in Black's position before committing ourselves.

Black develops naturally while defending against our e4 threat and preparing to castle kingside. The knight on f6 is flexibly placed and can reroute depending on how our position develops. This is solid development, though Black is still committed to the aggressive f5 pawn and hasn't resolved the tension in the center yet.

Now we strike at the center, challenging Black's f5 pawn directly—this is the logical follow-up to our setup, and it's why this line is called the Lisitsyn Gambit Deferred. We're willing to accept fxe4 because after dxe4, we maintain a strong pawn center and our pieces coordinate well. Black must decide whether to capture and weaken their kingside further, or retreat the pawn and allow us a dominating central presence.
